title: Workers comp tracking
content:
🧠 Around 14 hours. That is how long workers comp claims tracking took every month. Like 12-13 hours of just checking spreadsheets and sending follow-up emails.

Quick tip for staffing agencies:

Set up automatic status updates for your workers comp claims.

Why it works:
- Claims don't get forgotten in random email threads
- Adjusters stop emailing "what's the status?" every other day
- You actually know which claims need attention without digging through files

Unpopular opinion: Most agencies track claims like it's still 2005. Spreadsheet with dates. Email chain with 47 replies. Someone manually checking if adjuster responded.

Was working with a client last week who had 23 open claims. Manager couldn't tell me which ones were actually moving forward. Just knew she spent Friday mornings updating the tracking sheet.

Built something simple. Google Sheet pulls claim data. Apps Script checks for updates. Sends alerts when claim sits too long without movement. Takes like 5 minutes to set up per claim type.

Now manager sees dashboard. Red = stuck. Yellow = waiting on documents. Green = progressing. That simple.

The fix was embarrassingly simple. But the time saved? Around 10-12 hours monthly.

If it works, it works.

Anyone else still tracking claims manually in 2025?

title: Work order management
content:
📈 Broke a property manager's cost tracking trying to "simplify" it. Merged two spreadsheets that shouldn't have been merged. Took around 3 hours to untangle.

But that mess actually showed the real problem.

They were tracking work orders in one place. Costs in another. Property details somewhere else. Every month someone spent basically a full day matching everything up to see which properties were bleeding money.

The transformation:

BEFORE:
- Work orders in one system, invoices in another, manual matching every month
- No idea what maintenance actually costs per property until 30 days later
- "Which building is over budget?" = 4 hours of spreadsheet work

AFTER:
- Work order created, cost gets tagged to the property automatically
- Real-time view of what each building is spending this month
- Budget alerts before things get out of control

The difference? They caught a plumbing issue at one property that was costing 3x normal. Would've kept bleeding for months.

Most businesses don't need AI for this stuff. Just need the data to stop living in 5 different places.

This keeps coming up in conversations lately.

Dealing with this too? Let's fix it 👉 https://lnkd.in/g5Es8BHP

title: Work order management
content:
🔥 Like 4-5 hours every week. That's how long my client spent just coordinating work orders with vendors. Calling, texting, emailing the same updates.

⚡ Challenge for property managers this week:

Pick ONE maintenance issue from last month. Count how many messages you sent about it.

Why?
Because you probably texted the vendor 3 times, called twice, and still had to follow up

💚 That back-and-forth kills your day

The expectation? Submit work order, vendor shows up, problem solved.

The reality? Vendor needs building access. Tenant isn't home. Parts are backordered. You're playing phone tag for a week.

⛔ We've been doing it this way because... actually, why?

Built a simple system for a client last month. Work orders automatically include all the details vendors actually need. Building codes, tenant contact info, photos. Updates go to everyone at once.

🧐 Who's counting their vendor messages this week? 🙋

Dealing with this too? Let's fix it 👉 https://lnkd.in/g5Es8BHP

title: Virtual time clock systems
content:
Broke their whole time tracking system trying to "upgrade" it. Had to rebuild from scratch. Manager was... let's say she had words for me.

I used to think GPS tracking for workers was overkill. Like, just trust people to clock in honestly, right?

Then I saw the Friday night ritual at this staffing company. Manager staying late every week, fixing timesheets. People clocking in from home. Buddy punching everywhere. Payroll disputes every single week.

So basically what I built - time clock that checks GPS when you punch in. Takes your photo. Creates geofence around job sites. If you try to clock in from wrong location, system sends alert.

Sounds simple but took me like 3 attempts to get geofence radius right. First version was way too tight - people couldn't punch in from parking lot. Also drained phone batteries because GPS was checking constantly. Had to optimize that part.

One worker complained system was "spying" on him. That was awkward. Had to explain it only checks location at punch time, not tracking you all day.

But the result? Buddy punching dropped to zero. Payroll disputes down like 90%. Manager finally got her Fridays back.

The thing is - you can teach someone to code. But you can't teach them why staffing companies have these weird problems. Industry knowledge beats fancy syntax every time.

Sometimes the fix is embarrassingly simple. Finding the right fix? That's the hard part.

Happy to chat if you are dealing with the same thing 👉 https://lnkd.in/g5Es8BHP
